perturbation.original_text: Affiliates (as such term is defined in Rule 12b-2 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended) or divisions (whether prepared by the Company, its advisors or otherwise) that is furnished on or after the date hereof to you by or on behalf of the Company (herein collectively referred to as the “ Evaluation Material ”) in accordance with the provisions of this letter agreement and to take or abstain from taking certain other actions herein set forth.
perturbation.changed_text: Affiliates (as such term is defined in Rule 12b-2 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended) or divisions (whether prepared by the Company, its advisors or otherwise) that is furnished on or after the date hereof to you by or on behalf of the Company (herein collectively referred to as the “ Evaluation Material ”) without any obligation to comply with the provisions of this letter agreement and without taking or abstaining from taking any certain other actions herein set forth.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the Evaluation Material is furnished without any obligation, the contract now contradicts its core purpose of ensuring confidentiality and restricted use.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 12b-2 defines 'affiliate,' and the original contract correctly uses this definition within a framework designed to protect confidential information shared with affiliates. The modified text, however, removes the obligation to treat the Evaluation Material confidentially, thereby undermining the protections intended by referencing the affiliate definition in the first place and contradicting the purpose of the Exchange Act which aims to protect investors by ensuring fair and transparent markets, an aim that can be undermined by misusing inside information, including confidential Evaluation Material.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly removes the obligation to comply with the letter agreement's provisions, directly contradicting the original contract's purpose and undermining the intended confidentiality and restricted use of the Evaluation Material. The Law Explanation notes that this undermines the protections intended by referencing the affiliate definition and the purpose of the Exchange Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines terms related to the Securities Exchange Act, and while it doesn't directly address confidentiality, it underscores the importance of clear definitions within the regulatory framework, which is weakened by the removal of obligations in the changed text. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is not available.

perturbation.original_text: You hereby acknowledge that you and your Representatives are aware, and will advise your Representatives, that the Evaluation Material may contain material, non-public information about the Company and that United States securities laws prohibit any person who has material, non-public information about a company from purchasing or selling any securities of the Company while in possession of such information.
perturbation.changed_text: You hereby acknowledge that you and your Representatives are aware, and will advise your Representatives, that the Evaluation Material may contain material, non-public information about the Company and that United States securities laws encourage persons with material, non-public information about a company to purchase or sell any securities of the Company while in possession of such information.
perturbation.explanation: Reversing the intent of insider trading laws fundamentally contradicts securities regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. This includes trading on material, non-public information. The altered contract language directly contradicts this by stating that securities laws encourage insider trading.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts established securities laws. The original text correctly states that insider trading is prohibited. The changed text incorrectly states that securities laws 'encourage' insider trading. This reversal constitutes a direct contradiction of the legal principle prohibiting trading on material, non-public information, as explained in the Law Explanation which cites Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5.

perturbation.original_text: You agree that, for a period of one year from the date of this letter agreement, neither you nor any of your Affiliates who are provided with Evaluation Material or become aware of your discussions regarding the Possible Transaction will, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment or employ (a) any individual serving as an executive officer of the Company, or (b) any employee of the Company or any of its subsidiaries with whom you have had substantial contact, or who is specifically identified to you (other than through a general roster of employees, general employee equity and compensation data or other employee data provided in due diligence that is general in nature), during your investigation of the Company and its business in connection with the Possible Transaction, in each case without obtaining the prior written consent of the Company
perturbation.changed_text: You agree that, for a period of one year from the date of this letter agreement, neither you nor any of your Affiliates who are provided with Evaluation Material or become aware of your discussions regarding the Possible Transaction will, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment or employ (a) any individual serving as an executive officer of the Company, or (b) any employee of the Company or any of its subsidiaries with whom you have had substantial contact, or who is specifically identified to you (other than through a general roster of employees, general employee equity and compensation data or other employee data provided in due diligence that is general in nature), during your investigation of the Company and its business in connection with the Possible Transaction, in each case without obtaining the prior written consent of the National Labor Relations Board.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the requirement for consent from the Company to the National Labor Relations Board (NLRB), the contract interferes with the company's right to manage its employees and introduces an obligation not supported by law, which governs collective bargaining and unfair labor practices, not individual employment solicitations in this context.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 8 of the NLRA outlines unfair labor practices by employers and unions. Requiring the NLRB's consent for hiring executives or employees, as described in the modified text, is not within the NLRB's purview and is not an action covered by the NLRA, thus the modified text is not in accordance with this act. The NLRA protects employees' rights to organize and bargain collectively, not to regulate hiring decisions in the context of a confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Page Not Found | GovInfo Skip to main content Page Not Found Help Error occurred. The page you requested cannot be found. Please report this error to askGPO. Provide the following information to help us resolve this problem: the URL of the page you were trying to access, the steps you followed to produce the error, specific search or browse terms, and/or a screenshot of the page where the error occurred. Thank you for your patience. Homepage Search Tips Skip back to top
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the Company's consent with the NLRB's consent for hiring employees. The Law Explanation states that this "is not within the NLRB's purview and is not an action covered by the NLRA." The Perturbation Explanation states that this change "interferes with the company's right to manage its employees and introduces an obligation not supported by law." As such, the change introduces a requirement not supported by the NLRA, which the Law Explanation specifically says does not cover such actions.

perturbation.original_text: This letter agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of Delaware. The parties hereby irrevocably and unconditionally consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Chancery Courts in the State of Delaware and the United States District Court for the District of the State of Delaware for any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this letter agreement or the Possible Transaction, and agree not to commence any action, suit or proceeding related thereto except in such courts.
perturbation.changed_text: This letter agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the People's Republic of China. The parties hereby irrevocably and unconditionally consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Arbitration Courts in Beijing, China for any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this letter agreement or the Possible Transaction, and agree not to commence any action, suit or proceeding related thereto except in such courts.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law and jurisdiction to the People's Republic of China (PRC) could render the contract unenforceable in the United States, especially if the agreement involves US-based companies and assets. It also imposes legal standards and judicial processes that conflict with US law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of International Comity; Enforceability of Foreign Judgments
perturbation.law_explanation: The principle of international comity dictates the recognition and enforcement of foreign judgments in US courts, but only to the extent that the foreign court's laws and procedures are fair and reciprocal. Switching the jurisdiction to China raises concerns about fairness and reciprocity, potentially rendering the contract unenforceable in the U.S. This is because the legal and judicial systems of the PRC differ significantly from those of the US, and judgments from Chinese courts may not be automatically recognized or enforced in the United States, depending on the specific circumstances and the principles of comity.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies Delaware law and Delaware courts for dispute resolution. The changed text replaces this with the laws and courts of the People's Republic of China. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both indicate that this change introduces significant concerns regarding fairness, reciprocity, and enforceability in the United States, potentially rendering the contract unenforceable. This is because the legal systems differ significantly and judgments from Chinese courts may not be recognized or enforced in the US. Since the snippets are empty, the justification relies upon the explanations provided.
